schools.summary: The divine ordering of the camp — 'the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron' (the word coming after the numbering of ch1, as the congregation with the tabernacle was about to set forward, the counted army now to be arranged, Gill); a general appointment governing both encampment at rest and order of march (Henry); Moses commanded to teach the people how to pitch their tents and erect the ensigns of their fathers' houses, each knowing his place (Clarke).

Calvin: the distribution into separate bands served to prevent strife, for so natural is ambition to man that, had not God assigned each their proper position, they would have quarrelled for the place of honour; the divine arrangement forestalling envy, God a God of order not confusion.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron

schools.summary: The standards and the reverent distance — 'every man shall pitch by his own standard, with the ensign of their father's house: far off about the tabernacle' (four great standards or banners, each marking one of four camps of three tribes, distinguished by colours or figures, and besides these each father's house its own lesser ensign, every man knowing both tribe and family sign, Gill/Wesley); much pains taken by commentators to work out the encamping of so vast a host (Clarke); 'far off about the tabernacle' — pitching at a respectful distance, neither crowding nor leaving it, the space marking reverence for the holy dwelling (Wesley); the tabernacle in the midst, the Levites nearest, the twelve tribes in four camps beyond, God's dwelling the heart of the whole; the typology — the church ordered about God in its midst.

themes: the ensign of their father's house

schools.summary: Judah set first, on the east — 'on the east side toward the rising of the sun shall they of the standard of the camp of Judah pitch' (the east, the quarter of sunrise and the place reckoned 'before,' the front of the camp and head of the march, Judah leading the van from there, Gill); God himself appointing each tribe its place to prevent strife and envy, the order not of man's contriving (Henry); Judah's primacy — the most numerous tribe, but chiefly because Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Gen 49:9), was to descend from it, indeed from the loins of this Nahshon, in the genealogy of the Messiah (Mt 1:4, Wesley); the tribe from which the King would come set at the head, leading as Christ leads his church.

The muster of Judah — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred' (74,600); the recurring formula 'his host, and those that were numbered of them,' the army of the tribe according to its numbering, repeated for each tribe (Num 2:6ff), each tribe reckoned as a host under its captain (Keil and Delitzsch); the same count taken in the numbering of ch1, the camp built on the census (Gill); Judah's host the greatest in number, fitting the tribe at the head, the leading place matched by leading strength; the people reckoned not as a crowd but as marshalled armies, Israel going forth as the host of the LORD.

themes: threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred

schools.summary: Issachar set with Judah — 'those that do pitch next unto him shall be the tribe of Issachar: and Nethaneel the son of Zuar shall be captain' (Issachar taking his station next to Judah, one of three tribes under Judah's banner on the east); the fitness of the grouping — Issachar and Zebulun, the two tribes set with Judah, his full brothers, sons of the same mother Leah, fitting that those born of one mother encamp together, the bond of kinship strengthening the order (Gill); the captain Nethaneel the son of Zuar, prince of Issachar, appointed to lead his host; God's arrangement wise not arbitrary, joining kindred tribes that brotherly affection knit the camp and lessen strife; each tribe given its own prince and place, none neglected.

The muster of Issachar — 'his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were fifty and four thousand and four hundred' (54,400 fighting men, the same number taken in the census of ch1, the camp arrangement resting on the numbering already made, Gill); the recurring pattern — each tribe named, its captain appointed, its host counted, the enumeration deliberate and complete that every tribe's strength be known and place fixed; Issachar, though under Judah's lead, a great host in its own right, more than fifty thousand strong; the careful counting testifying to God's particular regard for his people, numbering them tribe by tribe as a shepherd his flock, none overlooked.

themes: fifty and four thousand and four hundred

schools.summary: Zebulun, third in the eastern camp — 'then the tribe of Zebulun: and Eliab the son of Helon shall be captain' (Zebulun pitching next, completing Judah's company); the order of the three not simply Judah, Issachar, Zebulun in a line, but Judah the leader in the midst of his standard, Issachar before him and Zebulun behind, the banner in the centre (Gill); Zebulun, like Issachar, a son of Leah, full brother to Judah, so the eastern camp wholly composed of Leah's sons, the children of one mother under one standard; the captain Eliab the son of Helon; every tribe with its fixed place and appointed leader, the vast camp arranged with the precision of a marshalled army; the grouping by mother binding the camp in natural unity.

themes: Judah in the midst of his standard

schools.summary: The muster of Zebulun — 'his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were fifty and seven thousand and four hundred' (57,400 fighting men, the same number as the census of ch1, Gill); Zebulun, the third of Judah's company, itself a strong host, second in the eastern camp only to Judah, the brother-tribes together a mighty array; the steady deliberate manner of the record — tribe by tribe, captain and number, the same form repeated that nothing be left uncertain in the ordering of the host; the careful enumeration bearing its own witness, every tribe owned, counted, and given its place, none too small to be reckoned; the eastern camp now complete the greatest of the four, fitting Judah at its head and the line of Christ.

themes: fifty and seven thousand and four hundred

schools.summary: The total of Judah's camp and its leading the march — 'all that were numbered in the camp of Judah were an hundred thousand and fourscore thousand and six thousand and four hundred... These shall first set forth' (186,400, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun together, the three brother-tribes of Leah by far the largest of the four camps, Gill); 'these shall first set forth' — the camp of Judah leading the way when the host moved, going before the tabernacle and the other tribes, the foremost place in the camp also foremost in the march; the fitness — the tribe of Judah, from which Christ the Lion would come, both encamping at the head and leading the host, the line of promise going before; the typology — as Judah led Israel, so Christ the true Judah leads his church.

themes: all numbered in the camp of Judah
an hundred fourscore and six thousand four hundred

schools.summary: Reuben's camp on the south — 'on the south side shall be the standard of the camp of Reuben... and the captain shall be Elizur the son of Shedeur' (the camp of Reuben, with Simeon and Gad, under Reuben's banner on the southern quarter, divided into lesser bodies or companies called armies, Gill); the place given to Reuben — though Reuben was Jacob's firstborn and might have claimed the foremost place, that honour had passed from him for his sin (Gen 49:4) to Judah, yet Reuben not cast off but given the leading of the second camp, next in dignity after Judah; God's ordering honouring Judah with the first place yet leaving the firstborn a place of leadership, none without honour while the line of promise held the chief room.

themes: the firstborn given the second place

schools.summary: The muster of Reuben — 'his host, and those that were numbered thereof, were forty and six thousand and five hundred' (46,500, the same number as the census of ch1); the distinction between 'host' and 'camp' — the host of Reuben the tribe's own army of 46,500, distinct from the 'camp' of Reuben, the whole company of three tribes (Reuben, Simeon, Gad) under his standard, each tribe a host, the three together a camp (Gill); Reuben, though leading the second camp, smaller in number than Judah, the firstborn's host not matching the royal tribe's, strength as well as honour having largely passed to Judah; each tribe great or small owned and counted in its place.

themes: forty and six thousand and five hundred

schools.summary: Simeon set with Reuben — 'those which pitch by him shall be the tribe of Simeon: and the captain shall be Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai' (Simeon taking his station next to Reuben, one of three tribes under his standard on the south); the fitness — Reuben and Simeon brothers by the same mother as well as father, both sons of Leah, so the southern camp too joining brothers of one mother that natural affection bind them and lessen strife, as Judah's camp gathered Leah's sons on the east (Gill); the captain Shelumiel the son of Zurishaddai; the consistent principle — kindred tribes encamped together, the camp knit by bonds of blood as well as ordered by divine command; Simeon, whose place at his father's blessing had been clouded (Gen 49:5), yet given his tribe, captain, and place, not cast off.

The muster of Simeon — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were fifty and nine thousand and three hundred' (59,300 fighting men, the same number as the census of ch1, Gill); Simeon, though set under Reuben's lead, in fact the more numerous tribe, mustering more men than Reuben its leader, the place of leadership not always given to the largest host but ordered by God on other grounds, here Reuben's right as firstborn; the plain deliberate recording — each tribe named, captain appointed, number told, the form unvaried, that the whole host be set in exact order; the counting owning the tribe and giving it its place, God numbering each as he sees fit; the southern camp gathering strength, Reuben and Simeon together above a hundred thousand.

themes: fifty and nine thousand and three hundred

schools.summary: Gad, third in the southern camp — 'then the tribe of Gad: and the captain of the sons of Gad shall be Eliasaph the son of Reuel' (Gad set on the other side of Reuben's standard from Simeon, so that the leader Reuben lay in the midst, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre, Gill); the fitness — Gad the eldest son of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid, fitly placed under the standard of Leah's own sons Reuben and Simeon, the handmaid's son joined to the mistress's sons, the household of Leah kept together; the captain Eliasaph the son of Reuel; the tribes grouped by the ties of their mothers, that affection and kinship bind the camp; Gad, though sprung from a handmaid, given full place and honour, his own captain and station, none despised.

themes: the handmaid's son with Leah's sons

schools.summary: The muster of Gad — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty and five thousand and six hundred and fifty' (45,650 fighting men, the same number as the first chapter's census, Gill); the host of Gad distinct from those of Reuben and Simeon, the three together making up the one camp of the south, each tribe its own army, the three one company under Reuben's standard; Gad, though the smallest of the three southern tribes, yet a strong host of more than forty-five thousand, counted with the same care as the greater, the handmaid's tribe reckoned no less exactly than the sons of Leah; the unbroken pattern — tribe by tribe, captain and number, the whole host ordered to a man; God's regard for each part of his people, none overlooked.

themes: forty and five thousand six hundred and fifty

schools.summary: The total of Reuben's camp and its place in the march — 'all that were numbered in the camp of Reuben were an hundred thousand and fifty and one thousand and four hundred and fifty... And they shall set forth in the second rank' (151,450, Reuben, Simeon, and Gad together, the second-largest of the four camps after Judah's, Gill); 'set forth in the second rank' — the camp of Reuben following next after Judah when the host moved, the order of the camp also the order of the march, each camp moving in its appointed turn; the tabernacle and Levites going between this camp and the two that followed, the holy things carried in the midst; not only where each tribe pitched but when each camp moved fixed by God, that the multitude march without confusion; the ranks set by divine appointment, not seized by the strongest.

themes: all numbered in the camp of Reuben
an hundred fifty-one thousand four hundred and fifty
set forth in the second rank
God sets the order of march

schools.summary: The tabernacle and the Levites in the midst — 'the tabernacle of the congregation shall set forward with the camp of the Levites in the midst of the camp' (God's dwelling the centre of the whole array, the four camps about it on the four sides, the Levites immediately around it, the tabernacle moving in the middle, two camps before and two behind, Gill); 'in the midst' not to be taken with strict exactness, for in the march the Levites and holy things were divided, part going after Judah and part exactly in the centre (Num 10:17), yet in the main the sanctuary carried in the heart (Wesley); 'as they encamp, so shall they set forward' — the same order at rest and on the march, no man leaving his standard; the great truth — God dwelling in the midst of his people, his sanctuary their centre and defence; the typology — so God dwells in the midst of his church.

themes: the tabernacle in the midst of the camp
with the camp of the Levites
as they encamp, so shall they set forward

schools.summary: Ephraim's camp on the west — 'on the west side shall be the standard of the camp of Ephraim... and the captain shall be Elishama the son of Ammihud' (the camp of Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin — the children and grandchildren of Rachel — under Ephraim's banner on the western quarter, behind the tabernacle, in the rear, Gill/Keil); Ephraim's precedence — though the younger son of Joseph, set before his elder brother Manasseh, given the standard while Manasseh pitched by him, in fulfilment of dying Jacob's prophecy, who crossed his hands and set the younger before the elder, foretelling Ephraim the greater (Gen 48:19, Wesley); God's ordering making good the old word of blessing, the younger preferred by sovereign grace; the western camp of Rachel's line keeping the grouping by mother.

The muster of Ephraim — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty thousand and five hundred' (40,500, the same number as the census of ch1); Ephraim, though set above Manasseh and given the leading of the western camp by the old prophecy, not the largest tribe, the precedence resting not on numbers but on the word of blessing and the sovereign choice of God who exalts the younger and lesser as he wills; the recurring formula 'his host, and those that were numbered of them' kept for every tribe alike, each reckoned as an army in its place (Keil and Delitzsch); Ephraim leading the rear camp with an army of forty thousand, substantial though smaller than the leaders of the eastern and southern camps; grace and not strength setting the order.

themes: grace not strength sets the order

schools.summary: Manasseh set with Ephraim — 'by him shall be the tribe of Manasseh: and the captain shall be Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur' (Manasseh pitching next to Ephraim, one of three tribes under his standard on the west); the striking submission — though Manasseh was the elder brother, the firstborn of Joseph, he was given no standard of his own but directed to pitch by Ephraim's, the younger preferred before him, agreeably to Jacob's prophecy who set the younger above the elder (Gen 48:19, Gill); the captain Gamaliel the son of Pedahzur; Manasseh, though second to his younger brother, not slighted but keeping his place, captain, and honour, the prophecy ordering the precedence but not casting off the elder; God's arrangement quietly fulfilling the words of the patriarchs.

themes: by him the tribe of Manasseh
the prophecy fulfilled in the camp

schools.summary: The muster of Manasseh — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were thirty and two thousand and two hundred' (32,200 fighting men, the same number as the census of ch1, Gill); Manasseh the smallest of all the twelve tribes at this numbering, fewer in men than any other, yet counted and given its place with the same care as the greatest, none too small to be owned in the host of the LORD; Manasseh, set under his younger brother Ephraim and the least in number, doubly humbled in the order of the camp, yet given its captain, standard-fellowship, and station; the careful counting of the least tribe showing God values each part of his people, the small no less than the great; strength and number not determining honour in God's ordering.

themes: thirty and two thousand and two hundred

schools.summary: Benjamin, third in the western camp — 'then the tribe of Benjamin: and the captain shall be Abidan the son of Gideoni' (Benjamin under Ephraim's standard, on the side opposite Manasseh, the leader in the midst, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre, Gill); the fitness — Benjamin the son of Rachel, as Joseph the father of Ephraim and Manasseh was, so the western camp gathering the house of Rachel — her son Benjamin and her grandsons Ephraim and Manasseh — under one standard, the children of one mother kept together as the camps of Leah's sons had been; the captain Abidan the son of Gideoni; Benjamin, the youngest of Jacob's sons, given full place and honour, encamped with his nearest kin; the western camp complete in its three tribes, all of Rachel's line.

The muster of Benjamin — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were thirty and five thousand and four hundred' (35,400 fighting men, the same number as the first chapter's census, Gill); Benjamin, the youngest tribe, yet a host of more than thirty-five thousand, larger than his kinsman Manasseh, counted with the same exactness as the rest; the western camp of Rachel's line the smallest of the four, its three tribes together fewer than any other camp, yet each owned and numbered, the lesser camp reckoned as carefully as the greater; the unbroken pattern — tribe by tribe, captain and number, the whole host ordered to a man; the enumeration testifying that God knows and numbers all his people, the least no less than the greatest.

themes: thirty and five thousand and four hundred

schools.summary: The total of Ephraim's camp and its place in the march — 'all that were numbered of the camp of Ephraim were an hundred thousand and eight thousand and an hundred... And they shall go forward in the third rank' (108,100, Ephraim, Manasseh, and Benjamin together, the smallest of the four camps, nearly eighty thousand fewer than Judah's great host, Gill); 'go forward in the third rank' — the camp of Ephraim following in the third place when the host moved, marching after the tabernacle and its Levites; two camps (Judah and Reuben) before the sanctuary, the tabernacle in the midst, two camps (Ephraim and Dan) behind, Ephraim leading the rearward division; the smallest camp keeping its appointed rank, neither hurried nor left behind, God assigning its place by appointment not by strength.

themes: all numbered of the camp of Ephraim
an hundred and eight thousand and an hundred
go forward in the third rank
the smallest camp keeps its rank

schools.summary: Dan's camp on the north — 'the standard of the camp of Dan shall be on the north side... and the captain shall be Ahiezer the son of Ammishaddai' (the camp of Dan, Asher, and Naphtali under Dan's banner on the northern quarter, the left wing of the grand army, over against Reuben's camp on the south, Gill); the dignity given to Dan — son of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid, yet given a standard of his own and the leading of a whole camp, the firstborn of the sons of the handmaids, his tribe numerous and warlike, fit to lead; the grouping by mother — Dan's camp gathering the sons of the handmaids, Dan and Naphtali (Bilhah) and Asher (Zilpah), the children of the bondwomen encamped together; the whole twelve ordered by the ties of their mothers; with Dan on the north the four sides manned, the camp complete about God's dwelling.

The muster of Dan — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were threescore and two thousand and seven hundred' (62,700, the same number as the census of ch1); Dan, though sprung from a handmaid, the second most numerous tribe in all Israel, exceeded only by Judah, so great a host fitly leading the northern camp and bringing up the rear of the whole march where the danger was; the recurring formula kept for every tribe alike, each reckoned as an army in its place (Keil and Delitzsch); the providence — the handmaid's son made so numerous and strong, given the leading of a camp and the guarding of the rear, God exalting the lowly and giving honour to the tribe that birth might have slighted; Dan's strength matched to its station.

themes: threescore and two thousand and seven hundred

schools.summary: Asher set with Dan — 'those that encamp by him shall be the tribe of Asher: and the captain shall be Pagiel the son of Ocran' (Asher taking his station next to Dan, one of three tribes under his standard on the north); the fitness — Dan having a standard of his own though the son of a handmaid, the firstborn of the sons of the handmaids, his tribe warlike and numerous, and Asher too a handmaid's son (of Zilpah, Leah's maid), fitly joined under Dan's standard, the camp gathering the children of the bondwomen together as the other camps gathered the sons of the freeborn wives (Gill); the captain Pagiel the son of Ocran; the tribes grouped by the ties of their mothers, that kinship bind each camp, none despised for their birth but given full place, standard, and captain.

The muster of Asher — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were forty and one thousand and five hundred' (41,500 fighting men, the same number as the census of ch1, Gill); Asher, a handmaid's son, yet a host of more than forty thousand, counted with the same care as the freeborn tribes, the children of the bondwomen reckoned no less exactly than the sons of Leah and Rachel; the plain deliberate recording — each tribe named, captain appointed, number told, the whole host ordered to a man, none left uncounted; the counting owning the tribe and giving it its place, God numbering each, the handmaid's son as truly as the freeborn; the northern camp gathering strength, Dan and Asher together above a hundred thousand.

themes: forty and one thousand and five hundred

schools.summary: Naphtali, third in the northern camp — 'then the tribe of Naphtali: and the captain shall be Ahira the son of Enan' (Naphtali pitching by Dan's standard, on the side opposite Asher, the leader Dan in the midst, one tribe before and one behind, the banner in the centre, Gill); the fitness — Naphtali, like Dan, a son of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid, full brother to Dan, so the northern camp gathering the sons of the handmaids — Dan and Naphtali of Bilhah, Asher of Zilpah — the children of the bondwomen as one company under one standard; the captain Ahira the son of Enan, the last of the twelve princes; with Naphtali the whole twelve tribes now ordered, four camps of three about the tabernacle, every tribe grouped by the ties of its mother.

The muster of Naphtali — 'his host, and those that were numbered of them, were fifty and three thousand and four hundred' (53,400 fighting men, the same number as the first chapter's census, Gill); Naphtali, the last of the tribes to be counted in the camps, a strong host of more than fifty-three thousand, reckoned with the same care as the first, the twelfth tribe owned as fully as Judah the first; with Naphtali numbered, every one of the twelve tribes told in its place, the whole host reckoned tribe by tribe from Judah to Naphtali, none omitted; the unbroken pattern — name, captain, and number for each, the array complete and exact; God's regard for every part of his people, the whole multitude numbered, each man in his place.

themes: fifty and three thousand and four hundred

schools.summary: The total of Dan's camp and its rearguard place — 'all they that were numbered in the camp of Dan were an hundred thousand and fifty and seven thousand and six hundred. They shall go hindmost with their standards' (157,600, Dan, Asher, and Naphtali together, the largest of the four camps next to Judah's, Gill); 'go hindmost' — the camp of Dan bringing up the rear, marching last of the four camps; the wisdom — Dan's the strongest camp after Judah, fitly set in the rear as Judah the strongest marched in the front, the two mightiest camps at the head and foot of the column, that the host and the tabernacle in the midst be best guarded where the danger was greatest, before and behind (Wesley); God ordering the march so the strong led and the strong closed, the weaker camps and the holy things protected between; the rearguard a place of honour and trust.

themes: all numbered in the camp of Dan
an hundred fifty-seven thousand six hundred
the strong guard van and rear

schools.summary: The grand total of the host — 'all those that were numbered of the camps... were six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty' (603,550 fighting men, the four camps together, the twelve tribes apart from the Levites, the same grand total as the numbering of ch1, Keil and Delitzsch); the number taken by Moses and Aaron, assisted by the twelve princes of the tribes, now constituted captains over the hosts they had helped to number, the leaders of the census becoming the leaders of the camps (Gill); the greatness of the host — more than six hundred thousand men of war, besides women, children, and Levites, a mighty nation grown from the seventy souls that went into Egypt; the faithfulness of God, multiplying Abraham's seed as promised; the whole multitude reckoned to a man, none lost, all numbered.

themes: numbered by the house of their fathers
six hundred three thousand five hundred and fifty
the grand total of the host

schools.summary: The Levites set apart from the muster — 'the Levites were not numbered among the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses' (Levi not reckoned in the census of the fighting men but counted by itself, a camp of its own about the tabernacle, numbered separately by a different rule, Num 1:49, Gill/Keil); the ground 'as the LORD commanded Moses' — the separation by express divine appointment, not human choice, God having set Levi apart from the armies for the service of the sanctuary, to bear and tend the tabernacle and encamp immediately around it; the tribe given wholly to God's service not counted among the men of war nor marching in the ranks, its place the centre about God's dwelling; the typology — as Levi set apart for God, so God claims a people peculiarly his own, a holy priesthood.

Israel's full obedience — 'the children of Israel did according to all that the LORD commanded Moses: so they pitched by their standards, and so they set forward' (the people forming themselves into the four camps, so many tribes to a camp, a captain over each tribe and a standard to each camp, by these both pitching and marching exactly as commanded, nothing altered or neglected, Gill); both at rest and on the march each tribe and family keeping its place, the whole vast host moving and resting as one well-disciplined army; the lesson — the people, so often murmuring, here yielding a ready and full obedience, when God assigns each his place his people to keep it without envy; the ordered camp a thing of beauty and strength, 'How goodly are thy tents, O Jacob' (Num 24:5); the typology — the church keeping its appointed places about the God in its midst.

themes: did according to all the LORD commanded
